titel: Deprivation of liberty measures for children and young people.
html:
According to the current regulations, individuals who have committed a crime so serious before the age of 18 that a custodial sentence is warranted, are, as a general rule, sentenced to closed juvenile detention facilities instead of prison. Closed juvenile detention facilities are administered by the Swedish National Prison and Probation Service, and the convicted individual is placed in a designated juvenile facility. There is no possibility of parole from closed juvenile detention.

In the government proposal, it is suggested that children and young people, in cases where a custodial sentence cannot be avoided, should be sentenced to prison instead of closed juvenile detention. This proposal means that the Swedish Prison and Probation Service will be responsible for the execution of the sentence, and that closed juvenile detention will be phased out of the sentencing system.

Children sentenced to prison should, as a general rule, be placed in special units for children and young people, which should be specially adapted to meet the needs and rights of children.

By making the sentence a prison sentence, children and young people will also be subject to the system of parole. Upon release from prison, they will, as a starting point, be placed under supervision in order to provide them with the support and control they need. The proposal also suggests that the legislation governing the execution of sentences should be adapted in certain areas to meet the specific needs and rights of children.

The proposed amendments to the law are, in all essential respects, intended to come into force on July 1, 2026.

Proposal for a Parliamentary Decision

Prop. 2025/26:132

Government's Proposals:

  1. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Code of Judicial Procedure.
  2. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Penal Code.
  3. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Social Security Code.
  4. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1946:804 on the introduction of the new Code of Judicial Procedure.
  5. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1971:289 on General Administrative Courts.
  6. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Passport Act of 1978:302.
  7. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Police Act of 1984:387.
  8. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1988:688 on Contact Bans.
  9. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1990:52 on Special Provisions for the Care of Young People.
  10. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1991:1129 on Psychiatric Care.
  11. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1998:603 on the Execution of Closed Youth Detention.
  12. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed repeal of the Act of 1998:603 on the Execution of Closed Youth Detention.
  13. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1998:620 on Criminal Record Checks.
  14. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 1998:714 on Compensation for Deprivation of Liberty and Other Coercive Measures.
  15. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Public Access and Secrecy Act of 2009:400.
  16. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Prison Act of 2010:610.
  17. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Education Act of 2010:800.
  18. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2015:96 on the Recognition and Enforcement of Deprivation of Liberty within the European Union.
  19. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2018:1250 on the Crediting of Time Served for Deprivation of Liberty.
  20. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2018:1251 on Sentencing.
  21. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2018:1693 on the Police's Processing of Personal Data within the Scope of the Criminal Data Act.
  22. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2018:1694 on the Customs Authority's Processing of Personal Data within the Scope of the Criminal Data Act.

7

Prop. 2025/26:132

  1. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2022:482 on Electronic Communication.
  2. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Act of 2024:326 on Secret Coercive Measures for the Purpose of Enforcing Deprivation of Liberty.
  3. The Parliament shall approve the government's proposed amendment to the Social Services Act of 2025:400.

8
tldr: The government wants to change the rules for young people who commit serious crimes. Instead of the current juvenile justice system, the proposal is that young people should be sentenced to prison, but with special accommodations tailored to their needs.
lattlast:
Today, young people who commit serious crimes typically receive a form of care called closed youth care. This care is managed by the Swedish National Agency for Institutions and Welfare and takes place in special youth homes. In the current system, it is not possible to be released on parole, which means that the entire sentence must be served.

The government is now proposing a change to this. They want young people who commit serious crimes to instead be sentenced to prison. If this is approved, the Swedish Prison and Probation Service will take over responsibility for carrying out the sentences. This also means that the old type of youth care will be phased out.

To protect the rights of children, the government proposes that those in prison be placed in special units. These units should be adapted to the needs of children and young people. The new system also means that it will be possible to be released on parole, which means that individuals may be released early under supervision to receive support and monitoring when they return to society.

The new laws are proposed to come into effect on July 1, 2026. The proposal also includes many changes to various laws, such as the Police Act, the Education Act, and the Social Services Act, to ensure that everything works together with the new rules.
